content: PD treatment is symptomatic, tailored to age, symptom severity, and cognitive status. Levodopa/carbidopa is most effective for motor symptoms; start low (25/100 mg TID) and titrate. Long-term use causes motor complications: wearing-off, dyskinesias, on-off fluctuations. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole, rotigotine) useful in younger patients (<65) to delay levodopa but less effective; side effects include impulse control disorders, somnolence, edema. MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline, selegiline, safinamide) provide mild symptomatic benefit, may reduce off-time. COMT inhibitors (entacapone, opicapone) extend levodopa effect for wearing-off. Amantadine treats dyskinesia and mild symptoms. Apomorphine (subcutaneous/inhaled) for rescue from off episodes. Advanced therapies: deep brain stimulation (DBS) of subthalamic nucleus or GPi for motor fluctuations/dyskinesia refractory to medical therapy (requires good levodopa response, no significant cognitive impairment); levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel infusion; focused ultrasound thalamotomy for tremor. Non-motor symptom management: antidepressants for depression, melatonin/clonazepam for REM sleep behavior disorder, cholinesterase inhibitors for dementia, botulinum toxin for sialorrhea.
